column1: Follow Up - Section Removal vs Control

We're adding a free shipping notification bar at the top of the cart experience. For sample pack customers (who don't qualify for free shipping), the bar will inform them they can upgrade to the starter kit to receive free shipping, with a button linking back to product selection. For starter kit customers (who already qualify), it will confirm their free shipping benefit to reinforce their purchase decision.

column1: Social Proof Counter with Velocity Indicators
column4:
"Real-time" activity signals tap into herding behavior psychology. The test replaces the static user review above the title and tests it with:
A) Live counter showing "X people started their EarlyBird ritual this week"
B) Conversion-focused statistic ""2 out of 3 people who try EarlyBird become subscribers! :star:"
C) Savings Callout: "EarlyBirds save an average of $87/month on coffee shop visits! :moneybag:
D) "Join 50,000+ morning optimizers who start their day with intention! :sun_with_face: The sunrise club that's changing how the world wakes up."

column1: Social Proofing + CTA add to HP Banner
column4: Currently HP Banner has a nice video but is lacking any form of CTA or social proofing. Adding a CTA while highlighting benefits (Made-in-America, 60 Day Guarantee, Free Shipping) and a carousel for reviews.

column1: Test removal of different sections of LP
column4:
Because of the length of the LP, there's a high chance that some of the sections added are actually hurting conversion. So we're testing out removing pieces of the LP one at a time in the variants to see which sections should be removed for maximizing conversion.

Removing different 6 sections within the LP:
1. 5 Reasons
2. What to look forward  to
3. How to use earlybird
4. Here's Where The Magic Happens
5. Morning cocktail
6. Earlybird vs Others

Price framing around daily use creates reference points that make premium products feel more accessible. Per-morning framing typically reduces price objections.

Adding text underneath the title to compare with Starbucks, then adjusting pricing to be more streamlined, and having the serving price within the add-to-cart button.

column3: If we add a short video or "sound bar" comparing a standard vibrator's noise to the Triple Vibe's silent motor, then CVR will increase among users living with roommates or partners.
column4: "How loud is it?" is a top-3 hidden objection. Proving the silence through a visual decibel meter or video demonstration removes that specific barrier by providing tangible proof.
column10: A "Play Sound" button next to a decibel graphic showing the Triple Vibe operates at <40dB (whisper quiet). Use a wave-form animation to visualize the low frequency. *Second Variant with Comparison

column3: If we add "Plain Packaging" and "Warranty" icons directly under every primary CTA button, then the CTR will increase because we are handling objections at the moment of peak friction.
column4: Users often hover over a button and think "Will my roommate see the box?" or "What if it breaks?". Answering these under the button prevents them from leaving to find the shipping policy.
column10: Small, monochromatic icons (box, shield, truck) with 2-word labels placed 10px below the main buttons.

column3: If we replace the small magnifying glass icon with a full-width search input field that includes placeholder text like "Search for a sensation (e.g., Suction)", then search usage and conversion will increase because users can find specific needs without digging through menus.
column4: Search users typically convert at 2-3x the rate of browse users. On mobile, the current search icon is a small target; making it an open field encourages high-intent "power users" to act.
column10: Move search from a hidden icon to a permanent bar at the top of the mobile screen. Use a light grey background for the input and a clear placeholder: "Find your pleasure...".

column3: If we change CTA copy to first-person ("I Want Triple Stimulation") instead of second-person ("Upgrade Your Pleasure"), then ATC will increase because it aligns with internal user desire.
column4: Psychological studies in CRO show that "I want..." statements reduce the "command" feel of a button and increase the user's sense of agency.
column10: Design Brief: No layout change. Update text on all primary buttons to: "I Want The Upgrade" and "I Want Triple Stimulation".

column3: If we add a persistent sticky footer on mobile with the price and a CTA, then cart additions will increase because the "path to purchase" is always available during a long-form scroll.
column4: Heatmaps on long-scroll pages often show a "dead zone" in the middle where users are convinced but cannot find the button, leading to bounce.
column10: A slim, bottom-anchored bar. Left side: Thumbnail of product + "£85 Upgrade". Right side: Purple button "Claim My Discount".
column11: Trigger bar visibility only after the user scrolls 15% down the page. Ensure it does not overlap with Live Chat icons.

column3: If we insert a CTA block after every primary feature description (Licking, Thrusting, Silent Tech), then the Add-to-Cart rate will increase by capturing impulse at the point of highest excitement.
column4: Currently, the page makes the user read the entire "story" before offering a button. This test treats each feature as a standalone reason to buy.
column10: Simple, clean buttons placed directly beneath each feature paragraph. Text: "Get this Upgrade".

column3: If we show a 5-second loop of a plain, unbranded brown box being delivered and opened to reveal the Triple Vibe, then Cart Abandonment will decrease.
column4: Users fear a giant "VIBRATOR" logo on their doorstep. Showing the literal brown box removes the "Delivery Anxiety" that kills sales at the final step.
column10: A high-quality looping GIF or video. Frame 1: A plain brown box on a doorstep (no logos). Frame 2: Box opens to reveal a sleek, premium Cosara box inside. *Create a second variant where the GIF is placed in "Is your packaging discreet" question itself

column3: If we replace the generic hero text with three clear segment buttons (For Her, For Him, For Couples), then Bounce Rate will decrease because users are immediately directed to relevant inventory.
column4: Analytical data suggests Cosara has three distinct audiences. Forcing them all through the same general browsing experience creates friction and "noise."
column10: Hero background image stays, but overlay 3 equal-width transparent tiles with bold headers: "SHOP FOR HER", "SHOP FOR HIM", "SHOP FOR COUPLES".

column4: Your founder identified that the current UI makes it hard to order more than 1 unit, and Ron noted that competing brands focus heavily on "packs".
column10: Redesign the PDP buy box to feature three large, selectable blocks above the "Add to Cart" button. Block 1: "1 Pair", Block 2: "3 Pairs (Most Popular)", Block 3: "6 Pairs (Best Value)".

column3: Adding a technical trust bar highlighting medical-grade quality will validate the premium price point for both healthcare pros and new demographics (travelers/athletes).
column4: Ron mentioned that their brand DNA is medical apparel. Highlighting "50k+ Nurses" and "12-hour comfort" anchors that quality.
column10: Add a clean, 3-icon grid immediately below the "Add to Cart" button. Use thin, medical-style icons. Icons: 1. "Medical-Grade Compression", 2. "Trusted by 50k+ Professionals", 3. "12hr Shift Comfort".

column1: Quantity Reward Offer Test (Gift vs. Discount)
column3: Testing a "Buy 4, Get 1 Free" or "Free Shipping" offer against the standard % discount will prove a higher emotional value, leading to more multi-unit orders.
column4: Your founder suggested changing quantity break rewards from discounts to physical rewards or shipping benefits to test AOV impact.
column10: Update the site-wide banner and the PDP quantity blocks to feature the "FREE" offer (e.g., "Add 3 Pairs for FREE Shipping").

column3: Adding a progress bar and "Quick Add" options for Navy, Black, and Caribbean Blue in the cart will nudge single-item buyers to complete a 3-pack set.
column4: The "Core 3" colors drive the majority of revenue. This cart logic leans into that existing behavior.
column10: Inside the slide-out cart, add a visual bar that fills as users add Core 3 items. Below the bar, show a "Missing these?" section with quick-add buttons for the other core colors.

column3: Introducing horizontal scrolling "bubble" navigation (Instagram-style) will allow users to quickly switch between sub-categories (Animals, Disney, Solids) without returning to the main menu, increasing session depth.
column4: Ron wants a way for users to "get it right away" when they land on a generic collection. This reduces the friction of finding specific print franchises like Disney.
column10: Add a horizontal scrolling row of circular "bubbles" at the top of all collection pages. Each bubble should have a high-quality product image and label: "Disney", "Solid Classics", "Animals", "Performance".

column3: Making the search functionality a permanent, high-contrast fixture in the header will speed up the path to purchase for returning users looking for specific SKU types.
column4: With multiple print franchises (like Disney) and various sock heights launching, users need a direct way to find specific items.
column10: Redesign the header to include a dedicated search bar (not just an icon) that is persistent on both desktop and mobile. Use placeholder text: "Search for Disney, Navy Scrubs, or Socks..."

column1: Mobile Navigation Category Tabs (Socks vs. Scrubs)
column3: By implementing a tabbed toggle at the top of the mobile menu to separate "Socks" and "Scrubs," we will reduce vertical scroll depth and friction, leading to a higher click-through rate to product pages.
column4: Ron identified the current navigation as "horrendous" and "really confusing". Mobile users are likely bouncing due to the length of the menu.
column10: Implement a high-contrast tabbed toggle at the very top of the mobile menu. Tab 1: "SOCKS", Tab 2: "SCRUBS". When "SOCKS" is active, show visual icons for product heights (Knee-High, Performance). When "SCRUBS" is active, show "Solid Classics" and "Printed Tops".

column3: Changing the hero CTA from “Shop Now” to “Get 20% Off Your First Order” will increase add-to-cart rate by 18% by creating stronger urgency and perceived value for new visitors.
column4: New visitors often hesitate with generic “Shop Now” CTAs due to lack of immediate incentive. A discount-focused message like “Get 20% Off Your First Order” creates perceived value, lowers perceived risk, and builds urgency for first-time buyers, likely lifting add-to-cart rate among cold traffic.

column3: Replacing the sticky “Add to Cart” button with a floating one that includes a mini product image + “Only 3 left!” stock warning will boost mobile conversions by 22% by reducing friction and adding scarcity FOMO.
column4: Mobile users frequently abandon carts when the sticky Add to Cart button feels disconnected from the product. Adding a floating button with a mini product image keeps visual context, while the “Only 3 left!” scarcity trigger creates FOMO and urgency. This combo reduces decision friction and boosts impulse conversions on mobile.

column10: READ ME ! >> IMAGE: https://bay-porte.com/site/assets/files/8569/blog_11.jpg TEXT: Every drop of water is a step towards joy for your furry friend. With our innovative cat water dispenser, you're not just providing fresh water; you're offering a lifeline to health and vitality. Cats, like the precious one you see, can often hide their discomfort. Dehydration is a silent ailment that leads to kidney disease, a common yet preventable condition in cats. Our dispenser ensures that your cat is enticed to drink regularly, reducing the risk of illness and restoring their zest for life. Because a hydrated cat is a happy cat, and nothing is more beautiful than your cat's health and happiness. Bring the purr back to their life with every refreshing sip.

column1: Sitewide Progress Bar: Tiered Reward Incentive
column3: If we implement a new dynamic announcement bar that tracks progress toward Free Shipping and a Free Shaker, then AOV and CVR will increase by creating a persistent secondary goal for the user throughout their entire browsing journey.
column4: Without an announcement bar, users may not realize they are close to a value threshold (like Free Shipping at 2 bags) until they reach the cart or checkout. By introducing a dynamic bar that visualizes progress toward $50 (Free Shipping) and $120 (Free Shaker), we gamify the site experience and incentivize users to add higher quantities to their cart to "win" the rewards.
column10: Inject New Component: Add a thin bar at the very top of the site (above the navigation).  Dynamic Text: Use a string that updates based on cart value: "Add $[X] for FREE Shipping" → "Add $[X] for a FREE Shaker."  Visual Progress: Include a thin brand-green progress line that fills as the cart value grows.  Reward 1 ($50): "FREE Shipping Unlocked! 🚚 Add $[X] more for a FREE Shaker!"  Reward 2 ($120): "CONGRATS! Free Shaker + Shipping Unlocked! 🎉"

column3: If we replace the static quantity selectors with a "Build a Bundle" experience and a dynamic gamification bar that unlocks tiered discounts (10% off at 3 bags, 15% off at 4+ bags) in addition to free shipping, then AOV and CVR will increase by incentivizing bulk purchases through a clear value exchange.
column4: The current buybox lacks a clear "reward" for adding more bags beyond the $50 shipping threshold. By introducing a progress bar that visualizes "Free Shipping" at 2 bags and subsequent tiered discounts, we leverage loss aversion to push users toward higher basket sizes.

Bundle Summary: Add a dynamic text line above the ATC button that reflects the current savings (e.g., "You're saving $12.00 on this bundle!").

column3: If we implement a "Build Your Bundle" progress bar, then Average Order Value (AOV) will increase by visually incentivizing users to hit discount tiers.
column4: Gamification leverages "completion bias," encouraging users to add more units to unlock savings. This aims to maximize revenue per visitor by making bulk purchasing more intuitive and rewarding than a standard selector.
column7: If we move core UVPs (20g Protein, 86 Superfoods, 0g Sugar) into the hero and replace the "All-In-One" section with a dedicated Founder Section, then CVR will increase by establishing celebrity authority and value above the fold.

column3: If we replace the horizontal sliding flavor selector with a vertical grid of "Visual Flavor Tiles" and switch from dropdowns to +/- quantity selectors, then CVR and ATC will increase by reducing the cognitive load required to choose and add flavors.
column4: The current horizontal slider hides flavors and uses high-friction dropdowns. A vertical grid makes all 7 flavors visible at once, and +/- buttons allow for faster quantity adjustments compared to a nested menu.
column10: Design Brief: * Variant A (Vertical Stack): Flatten the current slider into a vertical list. Replace flavor dropdowns with +/- quantity buttons. Variant B (Visual Tiles): Use a 2-column grid of circular "Flavor Tiles" (e.g., icons of strawberries, chocolate chunks) instead of product bag images. Use +/- buttons for quantity. Progress Bar: For both variants, move the "Free Shipping" tracker below the ATC for better visibility.

column4: Simplifying the cart by unifying its structure can enhance usability, leading to a smoother checkout process and higher conversion rates
column10: Hide the Toaster Cart (when adding to cart), show the Slideout Cart (when you click on cart), change Checkout button to Match PDP, Redesign Slideout Cart to look better. Hide the Chat button from covering the checkout button.

column4: Displaying estimated delivery dates sets customer expectations and can encourage quicker purchase decisions due to urgency.
Offering free shipping on all orders prominently in the topbar may reduce cart abandonment and increase overall conversion rates.
